Home / Legal / Custody & Visitation
Legal

Custody & visitation, in plain language.

What custody actually means, the arrangements courts tend to favor, and how to give yourself the best shot — no law degree required.

If you're reading this, you're probably somewhere between worried and overwhelmed. That's normal. Custody is one of the most stressful things a single dad goes through, but it gets a lot less scary once you understand how the pieces fit together. This is the plain-English version.

The single most important thing: courts decide custody based on the best interests of the child — not on the parent's gender. Being a present, stable, involved father is what counts, and it counts a lot.

The two kinds of custody

Almost every custody decision comes down to two separate questions, and you can have one without the other:

  • Legal custody — who gets to make the big decisions: schooling, medical care, religion. This is often shared ("joint legal custody") even when the kids live mostly with one parent.
  • Physical custody — where the kids actually live and sleep. This can be split many ways, from equal time to alternating weekends.

Common arrangements

There's no one "normal" schedule, but a few patterns come up again and again:

  • Joint physical custody — roughly equal time, often a 50/50 or 60/40 split. Increasingly common where both parents are stable and live close.
  • Primary custody with visitation — kids live mainly with one parent; the other has a set visitation schedule (every other weekend plus a weeknight is typical).
  • Sole custody — one parent has both legal and physical custody. Less common, usually tied to safety concerns.

How to give yourself the best shot

You can't control everything, but you can control how prepared and steady you look to a court:

  • Be involved now. School pickups, doctor's appointments, homework, bedtime. Document it.
  • Keep a calm paper trail. Save messages and keep a simple log of your time with the kids. Stay civil in writing — assume a judge may read it.
  • Have a real plan. Stable housing, a workable schedule, and childcare lined up.
  • Don't go it alone. Even one consultation with a family-law attorney can change how you approach the whole thing.

Can't afford a big firm? You have options — legal aid, pro se ("self-represented") resources, and low-cost clinics for dads. See the resources in the Legal section on the homepage.

A note on the hard days

Custody fights can make you feel like you're being judged as a father. You're not less of a dad because this is hard. Show up, stay steady, and get the right help — that's the whole game.

This page is a starting draft and is general information, not legal advice. Laws vary by state — talk to a qualified family-law attorney about your situation.
